questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of the Snowflake Native App Framework?

options.text: To package and distribute applications that run inside consumer Snowflake accounts

questions.explanation: The Native App Framework is for building, packaging, and distributing apps that consumers install in their own accounts.
questions.reference: Snowflake documentation: Native App Framework overview

questions.stem: Which provider-side object holds versioned artifacts for a native app?
questions.explanation: The application package stores versioned code and metadata before release.

questions.stem: Where does an installed native app run?

options.text: In the consumer Snowflake account boundary
In a Marketplace shared compute plane

questions.explanation: Native apps run in the consumer account, which is central to Snowflake’s isolation model.
questions.reference: Snowflake documentation: provider and consumer model

questions.stem: Why are references important in native apps?

options.text: They let consumers grant the app controlled access to consumer-side objects

questions.explanation: References are the supported mechanism for requesting access to consumer-owned objects.

questions.stem: What is setup.sql used for?

options.text: Running install or upgrade SQL needed to create app objects
Setting warehouse defaults for the whole account

questions.explanation: setup.sql is used during installation or upgrade to create required objects and configuration.

questions.stem: Why is running the app in the consumer account valuable?

options.text: Consumer data can stay within the consumer boundary
The app no longer needs permissions

questions.explanation: Consumer-account execution supports data isolation and governance.
questions.reference: Snowflake documentation: native app security model

questions.stem: Which technology is commonly embedded as the UI for a Snowflake native app?
questions.explanation: Streamlit is a common interactive UI layer for native apps.
questions.reference: Snowflake documentation: Streamlit in native apps

questions.stem: Why might a developer use st.cache_data carefully in a Streamlit app?

options.text: To improve responsiveness while managing freshness tradeoffs

questions.explanation: Caching can improve responsiveness, but developers still need to think about freshness.

questions.stem: What does Streamlit in Snowflake reduce compared with self-hosting a separate app tier?
questions.explanation: Hosting in Snowflake reduces external infrastructure to manage.

questions.stem: A Streamlit native app needs governed access to consumer data. What should the developer emphasize?

options.text: Using granted references and app roles
Uploading all consumer data to the provider first

questions.explanation: The app should use approved references and application roles rather than broad admin rights.
questions.reference: Snowflake documentation: Streamlit and app permissions

questions.stem: Which Snowflake capability is most directly used when a Streamlit app needs Python tabular processing?
questions.explanation: Snowpark provides DataFrame and procedural APIs that pair naturally with Streamlit apps.

questions.stem: Which file typically declares native app metadata and artifact locations?
questions.explanation: manifest.yml defines package metadata and artifact locations.

questions.stem: Where are code artifacts for a native app commonly stored before release?

options.text: In stages associated with the application package

questions.explanation: Application package artifacts are staged and versioned as part of the release workflow.

questions.stem: What should an upgrade script focus on?

options.text: Migrating app state and schema changes safely between versions
Copying all consumer data to the provider

questions.explanation: Upgrade scripts should preserve compatibility and migrate state as versions change.
questions.reference: Snowflake documentation: upgrades in native apps

questions.stem: Why is idempotent setup logic helpful in native apps?

options.text: It makes installs and upgrades more reliable if scripts rerun
It removes the need for references

questions.explanation: Idempotent scripts reduce install and upgrade risk by handling pre-existing state safely.
questions.reference: Snowflake documentation: native app setup best practices

questions.stem: What does an application role primarily do in a native app?

options.text: Expose app-specific privileges that consumers can grant to users

questions.explanation: Application roles define what users can do inside the installed application.

questions.stem: Where can providers list native apps for discovery by Snowflake customers?
questions.explanation: Marketplace is the distribution channel for discoverable app listings.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of a trial listing?

options.text: To let consumers evaluate an app before purchase or subscription
To disable all paid features permanently

questions.explanation: Trials give potential consumers a time- or feature-limited evaluation path.

questions.stem: Which monetization model might charge based on measured app usage?
questions.explanation: Usage-based pricing can align fees to app consumption metrics.

questions.stem: Why should providers understand listing review requirements before publishing?

options.text: Listings may need to satisfy packaging, security, and commercial review expectations
Review is only for free apps

questions.explanation: Publishing often involves review criteria around security, functionality, and listing completeness.

questions.stem: What does auto-fulfillment imply for a listing?

options.text: Eligible consumers can install without manual provider approval each time
The app needs no package version
All data leaves the consumer account

questions.explanation: Auto-fulfillment streamlines installation for approved listings.

questions.stem: Why are application roles important in native apps?

options.text: They let consumers grant only the privileges needed for app features
They force provider ownership of consumer data

questions.explanation: Application roles expose least-privilege access within the app.

questions.stem: If the app needs outbound calls to an external API, what should the provider do?

options.text: Use the supported external access pattern and request permissions explicitly
Open arbitrary internet access by default
Embed API keys in the listing description

questions.explanation: Outbound access requires explicit supported configuration rather than unrestricted internet access.
questions.reference: Snowflake documentation: external access from apps

questions.stem: Which statement best reflects the native app trust model?

options.text: Providers should request only minimal consumer permissions needed for features
Providers automatically receive all privileges in the consumer account
App roles are optional for serious apps

questions.explanation: Least-privilege permission requests build trust and fit Snowflake governance.
questions.reference: Snowflake documentation: native app security practices

questions.stem: What is the main risk of requesting excessively broad privileges in a native app?

options.text: Lower consumer trust and a larger blast radius than needed

questions.explanation: Overbroad privileges conflict with least-privilege expectations and may hinder adoption or review.
questions.reference: Snowflake documentation: least privilege and app review considerations

questions.stem: Which statement about provider visibility is most accurate?

options.text: Providers should not assume they can see arbitrary consumer data outside explicitly granted behavior
Marketplace analytics exposes every consumer row

questions.explanation: Providers should design with clear boundaries and not assume unrestricted consumer-data visibility.
questions.reference: Snowflake documentation: native app governance model

questions.stem: Why test a native app in a separate consumer-style environment?

options.text: To validate installation, permissions, and upgrades from the consumer perspective

questions.explanation: A consumer-like test environment exposes install-time and permission issues early.
questions.reference: Snowflake documentation: native app testing guidance

questions.stem: If setup.sql fails during install, what should developers inspect first?

options.text: Error output and SQL dependency ordering

questions.explanation: Install failures often stem from SQL errors or dependency ordering problems in setup scripts.

questions.stem: Why is automated testing valuable for Snowpark logic inside a native app?

options.text: It catches regressions before packaging and release
It removes the need for role design
It makes all upgrades backward compatible

questions.explanation: Automated tests improve release quality by catching breaking changes earlier.
questions.reference: Software testing practices for Snowpark apps

questions.stem: Which tooling is most likely to help during iterative app development?
questions.explanation: Snowflake CLI supports development, packaging, and execution workflows for apps.

questions.stem: What is the benefit of exercising upgrade tests with realistic consumer state data?

options.text: It reveals schema and migration issues absent in greenfield installs

questions.explanation: Upgrades often fail in stateful scenarios that clean installs never encounter.
questions.reference: Release engineering best practices for native apps
